title: Evaluating Generation Quality
concept: Generative models produce free-form text, making classification metrics useless; we must measure probability and fluency.

exercises.prompt: What makes generative models different from simple classification models?

options.text: They do not output any text at all.

exercises.explain: Generative models produce original, creative text outputs (like stories or emails) rather than just choosing a category label.

cards.title: Grading Creativity
cards.body: When an AI classifies an image, it's either right or wrong. But when an AI writes a story, there isn't just one right answer! How do we grade a computer's creative writing when there are infinite ways to say the same thing?

cards.title: Evaluating Generation Quality
cards.body: Evaluating generation quality means measuring how good, natural, and logical a model's generated text is. Since the model can write anything, we look at the mathematical probability of the generated words and how fluent (natural-sounding) the final sentences are.

cards.title: The Open-Menu Chef
cards.body: Imagine you are judging a cooking competition. If the chefs only make cheese pizzas, you can easily score them by counting how many toppings they got right. But if they can cook absolutely anything they want, you can't use a simple checklist. Instead, you have to taste-test the food to judge how delicious (fluent) it is and check if it actually makes sense as a meal!

cards.title: Quality in Action
cards.body: If an AI completes the sentence 'The sky is...', a high-quality model might output 'blue and clear' (highly probable and fluent). A low-quality model might output 'banana telephone' (low probability in this context) or 'blue the is sky' (terrible fluency).

cards.title: The 'One Right Answer' Myth
cards.body: Many people think that if an AI generates text that doesn't exactly match our human-written sample, the AI is wrong. In reality, there are millions of correct ways to rewrite the same sentence. Good evaluation tools look at general fluency and meaning, not just word-for-word matching!

cards.title: Classification vs. Generation
cards.body: See how grading changes when we move from simple classification to open-ended generation.

left.text: Checks if a label is correct or incorrect (e.g., 'Spam' or 'Not Spam').

right.text: Measures the likelihood of words and how natural the sentences sound.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we use simple classification metrics (like accuracy) to grade generative models?

options.text: Because they write free-form text, meaning there are many correct ways to say something.
Because generative models do not use math or statistics.
Because generative models are always 100% correct.
Because generative models can only output single numbers.

exercises.explain: Classification metrics like accuracy require a single correct label (like 'dog' or 'cat'). Generative models write free-form text, which has many different correct possibilities.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We can easily grade a story-writing AI using the exact same accuracy formulas we use for a simple spam-filter model.
exercises.explain: Since generative text is open-ended, we must use metrics that evaluate aspects like probability and grammatical flow instead of simple classification rules.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of generative evaluation.
exercises.sentence: Instead of looking for exact matching answers, we evaluate generative models by measuring word probability and grammatical [blank].
exercises.explain: Generative models need evaluation methods that measure how natural the text sounds (fluency) and how likely the words are to appear together (probability).

exercises.prompt: Which of these generated sentences demonstrates both high probability and high fluency?

options.text: 'The cat sat on the sky loudly ran.'
'The cat sat on the warm rug.'
'The cat sat on the the the the.'

exercises.explain: The second option represents high quality because it uses normal, likely word combinations (high probability) and makes grammatical sense (high fluency). The others contain nonsensical patterns.

exercises.prompt: How do we evaluate the quality of a generative text model if we cannot use simple classification metrics like accuracy?

options.text: We check if the exact words match a pre-written answer key.
We measure the mathematical probability of the words and evaluate their grammatical fluency.
We count the total number of words generated, regardless of what they mean.
We convert the text into numbers and check if the numbers are positive or negative.

exercises.explain: Because generative models produce creative, open-ended text, we cannot use simple right-or-wrong classification metrics. Instead, we use specialized metrics to measure how likely the words are (probability) and how natural the sentences sound (fluency).
